title: Simulation Verification of DKW Confidence Band Coverage
text: band simultaneous (1−α)(1-\alpha) coverage P(supx|F̂n(x)−F(x)|≤εn)≥1−α. P\!\left(\sup_{x} \lvert \hat{F}_n(x) - F(x) \rvert \leq \varepsilon_n\right) \;\geq\; 1 - \alpha. supremum equals Kolmogorov–Smirnov statistic DnD_n, need P(Dn≤εn)≥1−αP(D_n \leq \varepsilon_n) \geq 1 - \alpha. FF continuous, DnD_n distribution-free null distribution, coverage depend true distribution.

title: Simulation Verification of DKW Confidence Band Coverage
text: combination sample size nn nominal level 1−α1 - \alpha : Draw B=10,000B = 10{,}000 independent samples N(0,1)N(0, 1). Compute Dn=sup⁡x|F̂n(x)−F(x)|D_n = \sup_x \lvert \hat{F}_n(x) - F(x) \rvert via ks.test(). Compute εn\varepsilon_n using formula geom_ecdf(). Record fraction replications Dn≤εnD_n \leq \varepsilon_n.
code: set.seed(20240101)  B      <- 10000 ns     <- c(10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000) levels <- c(0.90, 0.95, 0.99)  eps_fn <- function(n, level) sqrt(log(2 / (1 - level)) / (2 * n))  results <- do.call(rbind, lapply(ns, function(n) {   do.call(rbind, lapply(levels, function(lv) {     eps <- eps_fn(n, lv)     dn  <- replicate(B, ks.test(rnorm(n), "pnorm", exact = FALSE)$statistic)     data.frame(       n         = n,       level     = lv,       nominal   = lv,       empirical = mean(dn <= eps)     )   })) }))

title: Simulation Verification of DKW Confidence Band Coverage
text: DnD_n continuous FF distribution-free, coverage holds continuous distribution. quick cross-check Uniform Exponential confirms : Coverage n = 100, level = 0.95 across distributions (B = 10000 sims). Coverage 95% distributions, confirming distribution-free guarantee.
code: set.seed(20240102)  n   <- 100 lv  <- 0.95 eps <- eps_fn(n, lv) B2  <- 10000  dists <- list(   Normal      = function() rnorm(n),   Uniform     = function() runif(n),   Exponential = function() rexp(n),   Beta        = function() rbeta(n, 2, 5) )  dist_results <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(dists), function(nm) {   rfun   <- dists[[nm]]   # Use the corresponding theoretical CDF   pfun   <- switch(nm,     Normal      = function(x) pnorm(x),     Uniform     = function(x) punif(x),     Exponential = function(x) pexp(x),     Beta        = function(x) pbeta(x, 2, 5)   )   dn <- replicate(B2, {     x  <- sort(rfun())     fn <- seq_along(x) / n     # Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic: max over pre- and post-jump     max(c(abs(fn - pfun(x)), abs((seq_along(x) - 1) / n - pfun(x))))   })   data.frame(distribution = nm, empirical = mean(dn <= eps)) }))  dist_results$nominal <- paste0(lv * 100, "%") dist_results$empirical_pct <- paste0(round(dist_results$empirical * 100, 1), "%") knitr::kable(dist_results[, c("distribution", "nominal", "empirical_pct")],              col.names = c("Distribution", "Nominal", "Empirical"),              align = "lcc",              caption = paste0("Coverage at n = ", n, ", level = ", lv,                               " across distributions (B = ", B2, " sims)."))

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: probability distribution fully characterized one standard functions: PDF ff, CDF FF, survival function S=1−FS = 1 - F, quantile function Q=F−1Q = F^{-1}, hazard function h=f/Sh = f / S. R, base distribution families provide (e.g. dnorm, pnorm, qnorm), user-defined distributions distributions packages may supply one two. ggfunction lets pass whichever function type whichever geom need. example, PDF want plot CDF, can write geom_cdf(pdf_fun = my_pdf) conversion happens automatically. vignette explains conversions available, work internally, accuracy expect.

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: probability geom accepts native function type via fun one alternate types via named parameters. table summarizes routes: Exactly one function source must provided; supplying one (e.g. fun pdf_fun) error.

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: Suppose PDF Gamma(2, 1) distribution: can plot PDF, CDF, survival function, quantile function without ever writing CDF quantile function :
code: my_pdf <- function(x, shape, rate) dgamma(x, shape = shape, rate = rate) gamma_args <- list(shape = 2, rate = 1) p1 <- ggplot() +   geom_pdf(fun = my_pdf, xlim = c(0, 8), args = gamma_args) +   ggtitle("PDF (native)")  p2 <- ggplot() +   geom_cdf(pdf_fun = my_pdf, xlim = c(0, 8), args = gamma_args) +   ggtitle("CDF (from PDF)")  p3 <- ggplot() +   geom_survival(pdf_fun = my_pdf, xlim = c(0, 8), args = gamma_args) +   ggtitle("Survival (from PDF)")  p4 <- ggplot() +   geom_qf(pdf_fun = my_pdf, args = gamma_args) +   ggtitle("Quantile (from PDF)")  (p1 | p2) / (p3 | p4)

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: Three internal helper functions perform numerical conversions. exported, understanding helps set expectations accuracy performance.

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: Given PDF ff, CDF F(x)=∫−∞xf(t)dt.F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt. Internally, pdf_to_cdf() evaluates integral point using R’s stats::integrate(), applies adaptive Gauss–Kronrod quadrature: evaluation point requires separate call integrate(), computationally expensive conversion. distributions known finite lower bounds (e.g. exponential), setting lower = 0 avoids integrating infinite domain improves speed accuracy. Accuracy. smooth, well-behaved densities absolute error typically 10−610^{-6}, well within plotting resolution. Densities singularities (e.g. Beta(0.5, 0.5) density, diverges 0 1) may produce larger errors near singular points.
code: pdf_to_cdf <- function(pdf_fun, lower = -Inf) {   function(x) {     vapply(x, function(xi) {       res <- try(         stats::integrate(pdf_fun, lower = lower, upper = xi,                          stop.on.error = FALSE),         silent = TRUE       )       if (inherits(res, "try-error")) NA_real_ else res$value     }, numeric(1))   } }

what: CDF to PDF: central finite differences
title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: Given CDF FF, PDF derivative f(x)=F′(x)f(x) = F'(x). Internally, cdf_to_pdf() approximates central difference: f(x)≈F(x+h)−F(x−h)2hf(x) \approx \frac{F(x + h) - F(x - h)}{2h} step size h=10−5h = 10^{-5}: fast naturally vectorized—single call evaluates grid points . Accuracy. central difference O(h2)O(h^2) truncation error, h=10−5h = 10^{-5} error roughly 10−1010^{-10} smooth CDFs. practice dominant error source floating-point cancellation F(x+h)≈F(x−h)F(x+h) \approx F(x-h), occurs extreme tails CDF nearly flat. harmless plotting density negligibly small regions.
code: cdf_to_pdf <- function(cdf_fun, h = 1e-5) {   function(x) {     (cdf_fun(x + h) - cdf_fun(x - h)) / (2 * h)   } }

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: Given CDF FF, quantile function solves F(x)=pF(x) = p probability p∈(0,1)p \(0, 1). Internally, cdf_to_qf() uses stats::uniroot() find root g(x)=F(x)−pg(x) = F(x) - p: algorithm starts initial search bounds [−10,10][-10, 10] adaptively doubles CDF values bracket target probability. handles distributions arbitrary support without requiring user specify bounds. boundary cases p=0p = 0 p=1p = 1 return −∞-\infty +∞+\infty directly. Accuracy. uniroot() converges machine precision (≈1.5×10−8\approx 1.5 \times 10^{-8}), quantile values effectively exact. main cost one CDF evaluation per uniroot() iteration (typically 10–20 iterations), applied independently probability point.
code: cdf_to_qf <- function(cdf_fun, search_lower = -10, search_upper = 10) {   function(p) {     vapply(p, function(pi) {       if (pi <= 0) return(-Inf)       if (pi >= 1) return(Inf)        lo <- search_lower; hi <- search_upper        # Adaptively widen bounds until they bracket the target       for (i in 1:25) {         if (!is.na(cdf_fun(lo)) && cdf_fun(lo) <= pi) break         lo <- lo * 2       }       for (i in 1:25) {         if (!is.na(cdf_fun(hi)) && cdf_fun(hi) >= pi) break         hi <- hi * 2       }        res <- try(         stats::uniroot(function(x) cdf_fun(x) - pi,                        lower = lo, upper = hi,                        tol = .Machine$double.eps^0.5),         silent = TRUE       )       if (inherits(res, "try-error")) NA_real_ else res$root     }, numeric(1))   } }

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: Given survival function S(x)=1−F(x)S(x) = 1 - F(x), CDF recovered simple subtraction: F(x)=1−S(x).F(x) = 1 - S(x). Internally, survival_to_cdf() wraps closure: Accuracy. conversion exact (floating-point rounding), errors order machine epsilon (≈2.2×10−16\approx 2.2 \times 10^{-16}).
code: survival_to_cdf <- function(survival_fun) {   function(x) 1 - survival_fun(x) }

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: Given quantile function Q(p)Q(p), CDF can recovered inverting relationship: evaluate QQ dense grid probabilities p1,…,pnp_1, \ldots, p_n get corresponding xx values, interpolate. Internally, qf_to_cdf() uses stats::approxfun() rule = 2 (constant extrapolation boundaries): grid avoids p=0p = 0 p=1p = 1 (map ±∞\pm\infty unbounded distributions). n=10,000n = 10{,}000 grid points, interpolation error typically 10−310^{-3} smooth distributions. Accuracy. Linear interpolation 10,00010{,}000 points gives roughly three decimal digits accuracy. sufficient plotting, though downstream differentiation (e.g. obtain PDF) amplifies error one order magnitude.
code: qf_to_cdf <- function(qf_fun, n = 10000) {   p_grid <- seq(1 / (n + 1), n / (n + 1), length.out = n)   x_grid <- qf_fun(p_grid)   stats::approxfun(x_grid, p_grid, rule = 2) }

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: Given hazard function hh, cumulative hazard H(x)=∫ℓxh(t)dtH(x) = \int_{\ell}^{x} h(t)\, dt, ℓ\ell support origin. default ggfunction uses ℓ=−∞\ell = -\infty; finite-support hazards, pass hf_lower plotting geom (example, hf_lower = 0 exponential Weibull hazards). Values finite hf_lower CDF, PDF, cumulative hazard equal 0. CDF follows F(x)=1−e−H(x)F(x) = 1 - e^{-H(x)}. Internally, hf_to_cdf() computes H(x)H(x) via stats::integrate() applies exponential transform: hf_to_chf() performs actual integration lower. Accuracy. Similar pdf_to_cdf, evaluation point requires call integrate(). Absolute error typically 10−610^{-6} smooth hazard functions.
code: hf_to_cdf <- function(hf_fun, lower = -Inf) {   chf_fun <- hf_to_chf(hf_fun, lower = lower)   function(x) 1 - exp(-chf_fun(x)) } hf_to_chf <- function(hf_fun, lower = -Inf) {   function(x) {     vapply(x, function(xi) {       if (is.finite(lower) && xi <= lower) return(0)       res <- try(         stats::integrate(hf_fun, lower = lower, upper = xi,                          stop.on.error = FALSE),         silent = TRUE       )       if (inherits(res, "try-error")) NA_real_ else res$value     }, numeric(1))   } }

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: Rather chaining hf_to_cdf + cdf_to_pdf (nests numerical operations can unstable), hf_to_pdf() applies identity f(x)=h(x)⋅e−H(x)f(x) = h(x) \cdot e^{-H(x)} directly: avoids finite-difference step entirely, producing accurate derived PDF. Accuracy. smooth hazard functions error comparable pdf_to_cdf—typically 10−610^{-6}.
code: hf_to_pdf <- function(hf_fun, lower = -Inf) {   chf_fun <- hf_to_chf(hf_fun, lower = lower)   function(x) {     vapply(x, function(xi) {       if (is.finite(lower) && xi < lower) return(0)       H_x <- chf_fun(xi)       if (is.na(H_x)) return(NA_real_)       hf_fun(xi) * exp(-H_x)     }, numeric(1))   } }

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: geoms require two conversions sequence. example, geom_qf(pdf_fun = ...) first integrates PDF obtain CDF (pdf_to_cdf), inverts CDF root-finding (cdf_to_qf). root-finding step calls derived CDF, calls integrate(). double nesting makes expensive path, cost still modest default n=101n = 101 evaluation points. Similarly, geom_survival(pdf_fun = ...) integrates PDF get FF, computes S(x)=1−F(x)S(x) = 1 - F(x). geom_hf() receives pdf_fun, derives CDF integration can compute h(x)=f(x)/(1−F(x))h(x) = f(x) / (1 - F(x)). new survival_fun qf_fun paths also produce chains: geom_pdf(survival_fun = ...) computes F=1−SF = 1 - S (exact), differentiates get ff. geom_pdf(qf_fun = ...) interpolates QQ get FF, differentiates. geom_qf(survival_fun = ...) computes F=1−SF = 1 - S, inverts root-finding.

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: can compare derived values exact analytic functions see conversion error practice. use standard normal distribution:  derived curves (black) visually indistinguishable exact curves (red dashed), confirming numerical methods accurate enough plotting.
code: # CDF derived from PDF vs exact p1 <- ggplot() +   geom_cdf(pdf_fun = dnorm, xlim = c(-3, 3)) +   geom_cdf(fun = pnorm, xlim = c(-3, 3), color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +   ggtitle("CDF: derived (black) vs exact (red)")  # PDF derived from CDF vs exact p2 <- ggplot() +   geom_pdf(cdf_fun = pnorm, xlim = c(-3, 3)) +   geom_pdf(fun = dnorm, xlim = c(-3, 3), color = "red", alpha = 0) +   ggtitle("PDF: derived (black) vs exact (red)")  # QF derived from CDF vs exact p3 <- ggplot() +   geom_qf(cdf_fun = pnorm) +   geom_qf(fun = qnorm, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +   ggtitle("QF: derived (black) vs exact (red)")  p1 | p2 | p3

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: Integration (pdf_to_cdf): O(n)O(n) calls integrate(), O(k)O(k) function evaluations kk depends integrand’s smoothness. Typically slowest single conversion. Differentiation (cdf_to_pdf): single vectorized evaluation—two CDF calls total. Essentially free. Root-finding (cdf_to_qf): O(n)O(n) calls uniroot(), requiring O(m)O(m) CDF evaluations (m≈15m \approx 15). Comparable integration cost. Survival CDF (survival_to_cdf): single vectorized evaluation—one subtraction per point. Essentially free. QF CDF (qf_to_cdf): One-time cost evaluating quantile function 10,000 grid points build interpolation table. setup, CDF lookups O(1)O(1) via approxfun(). Hazard CDF (hf_to_cdf): O(n)O(n) calls integrate(), comparable pdf_to_cdf. Use hf_lower finite-support hazards integration begins support origin. Hazard PDF (hf_to_pdf): O(n)O(n) calls integrate() plus one hazard evaluation per point. Slightly expensive pdf_to_cdf avoids instability chaining hf_to_cdf + cdf_to_pdf. Chained (pdf_fun quantile): O(n⋅m)O(n \cdot m) calls integrate(). expensive path still completes second default n=101n = 101. speed matters working distributions analytic forms multiple function types, supplying native function directly avoids conversion overhead. example, geom_cdf(fun = pnorm) faster geom_cdf(pdf_fun = dnorm), though produce visually identical output.

title: Cross-Conversion Between Distribution Functions
text: Discrete geoms (geom_cdf_discrete, geom_survival_discrete, geom_qf_discrete) perform exact arithmetic rather numerical approximation: PMF CDF: cumulative summation PMF values full integer explicit support. xlim can filter displayed range. exact floating-point rounding. PMF survival: cumulative summation followed S(x)=1−F(x)S(x) = 1 - F(x). exact. CDF quantile (discrete): CDF evaluated support point step function inverted directly. root-finding needed. Survival CDF (discrete): F(x)=1−S(x)F(x) = 1 - S(x) support point. Exact. Survival quantile (discrete): F(x)=1−S(x)F(x) = 1 - S(x) support point, invert. Exact. operations introduce approximation error, accuracy trade-discrete conversions.

code: set.seed(123) df <- data.frame(x = rnorm(40))  ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +   geom_eqf(color = "steelblue") +   geom_qf(fun = qnorm, args = list(mean = 0, sd = 1),            color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Empirical vs. theoretical quantile function",        subtitle = "Blue = data with DKW band, Red = N(0, 1)") +   theme_minimal() pp <- ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +   geom_ppplot(fun = pnorm) +   coord_equal() +   labs(title = "PP plot")  qq <- ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +   geom_qqplot(fun = qnorm) +   coord_equal() +   labs(title = "QQ plot")  pp + qq qq_black <- ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +   geom_qqplot(fun = qnorm, colour = "black") +   coord_equal() +   labs(title = "Black points")  qq_spectral <- ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +   geom_qqplot(fun = qnorm) +   scale_colour_gradientn(     colors = grDevices::rainbow(10),     limits = c(0, 1),     labels = function(x) paste0(round(100 * x), "%")   ) +   coord_equal() +   labs(title = "Spectral scale")  qq_black + qq_spectral

title: Introduction to ggfunction
text: ggfunction extends ggplot2 visualize mathematical functions directly. provides unified interface organized around two families: geom_function_1d_1d(): ℝ→ℝ\mathbb{R} \\mathbb{R} (scalar scalar) geom_function_1d_2d(): ℝ→ℝ2\mathbb{R} \\mathbb{R}^2 (parametric curves) geom_function_2d_1d(): ℝ2→ℝ\mathbb{R}^2 \\mathbb{R} (scalar fields, raster/contour) geom_function_2d_2d(): ℝ2→ℝ2\mathbb{R}^2 \\mathbb{R}^2 (vector fields) geom_pdf(): probability density function geom_cdf(): cumulative distribution function geom_pmf(): probability mass function (lollipop) geom_qf(): quantile function geom_cdf_discrete(): discrete CDF (right-continuous step function) geom_qf_discrete(): discrete quantile function (left-continuous step function) geom_survival_discrete(): discrete survival function S(x)=1−F(x)S(x) = 1 - F(x) (right-continuous step function) geom_survival(): survival function S(x)=1−F(x)S(x) = 1 - F(x) geom_hf(): hazard function h(x)=f(x)/S(x)h(x) = f(x)/S(x)

title: Learning Statistics with ggfunction
text: probability density function (PDF) describes relative likelihood continuous random variable taking given value. area curve interval equals probability falling interval. standard normal, P(−1<X<1)≈0.68P(-1 < X < 1) \approx 0.68:
code: ggplot() +   geom_pdf(fun = dnorm, xlim = c(-4, 4),            p_lower = pnorm(-1), p_upper = pnorm(1),            fill = "steelblue") +   labs(title = "Standard Normal Density",        subtitle = "P(-1 < X < 1) \u2248 0.68",        x = "x", y = "Density") +   theme_minimal()

title: Learning Statistics with ggfunction
text: cumulative distribution function (CDF) F(x)=P(X≤x)F(x) = P(X \le x) running area PDF −∞-\infty xx. Notice CDF’s steepest climb corresponds PDF tallest:
code: p1 <- ggplot() +   geom_pdf(fun = dnorm, xlim = c(-4, 4), fill = "steelblue",            p = 0.8) +   labs(title = "PDF", x = "x", y = "f(x)") +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot() +   geom_cdf(fun = pnorm, xlim = c(-4, 4), p = 0.8,            fill = "steelblue") +   labs(title = "CDF", x = "x", y = "F(x)") +   theme_minimal()  p1 + p2

title: Learning Statistics with ggfunction
text: quantile function Q(p)=F−1(p)Q(p) = F^{-1}(p) answers inverse question: given probability pp, value xx satisfies F(x)=pF(x) = p? median 50th percentile — Q(0.5)Q(0.5):
code: ggplot() +   geom_qf(fun = qnorm, args = list(mean = 0, sd = 1)) +   geom_vline(xintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "darkgreen") +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "darkgreen") +   labs(title = "Standard Normal Quantile Function",        subtitle = "Median at Q(0.5) = 0",        x = "p", y = "Q(p)") +   theme_minimal()

title: Learning Statistics with ggfunction
text: survival function S(x)=1−F(x)=P(X>x)S(x) = 1 - F(x) = P(X > x) gives probability surviving past time xx. hazard function h(x)=f(x)/S(x)h(x) = f(x) / S(x) instantaneous failure rate. exponential distribution constant hazard — memory aging. Weibull distribution allows increasing decreasing hazard depending shape parameter:  Blue = Exponential (constant hazard), Red = Weibull shape 2 (increasing), Green = Weibull shape 0.5 (decreasing).
code: p1 <- ggplot() +   geom_survival(cdf_fun = pexp, xlim = c(0, 6),                 args = list(rate = 0.5), color = "steelblue") +   geom_survival(cdf_fun = pweibull, xlim = c(0, 6),                 args = list(shape = 2, scale = 2), color = "firebrick") +   geom_survival(cdf_fun = pweibull, xlim = c(0, 6),                 args = list(shape = 0.5, scale = 2), color = "forestgreen") +   labs(title = "Survival Functions", x = "t", y = "S(t)") +   theme_minimal()  p2 <- ggplot() +   geom_hf(pdf_fun = dexp, cdf_fun = pexp, xlim = c(0.01, 6),            args = list(rate = 0.5), color = "steelblue") +   geom_hf(pdf_fun = dweibull, cdf_fun = pweibull, xlim = c(0.01, 6),            args = list(shape = 2, scale = 2), color = "firebrick") +   geom_hf(pdf_fun = dweibull, cdf_fun = pweibull, xlim = c(0.01, 6),            args = list(shape = 0.5, scale = 2), color = "forestgreen") +   labs(title = "Hazard Functions", x = "t", y = "h(t)") +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 3)) +   theme_minimal()  p1 + p2

title: Learning Statistics with ggfunction
text: skewed multimodal distributions, equal-tailed intervals waste coverage low-density areas. highest density region (HDR) always captures probable values given coverage level. Compare HDR equal-tailed interval right-skewed mixture:  HDR concentrates coverage high-density peak, equal-tailed interval extends far sparse right tail.
code: skewed <- function(x) 0.7 * dnorm(x, 2, 0.8) + 0.3 * dnorm(x, 5, 1.5)  p1 <- ggplot() +   geom_pdf(fun = skewed, xlim = c(-2, 10), shade_hdr = 0.9,            fill = "orchid") +   labs(title = "90% HDR", subtitle = "Shortest interval(s) covering 90%") +   theme_minimal()  # For comparison: equal-tailed 90% # Find quantiles by numerical inversion skewed_cdf <- function(x) {   0.7 * pnorm(x, 2, 0.8) + 0.3 * pnorm(x, 5, 1.5) } q_lower <- uniroot(function(x) skewed_cdf(x) - 0.05, c(-5, 10))$root q_upper <- uniroot(function(x) skewed_cdf(x) - 0.95, c(-5, 10))$root  p2 <- ggplot() +   geom_pdf(fun = skewed, xlim = c(-2, 10),            p_lower = 0.05, p_upper = 0.95,            fill = "steelblue") +   labs(title = "90% Equal-Tailed Interval",        subtitle = "Wastes coverage in the right tail") +   theme_minimal()  p1 + p2

title: Survival Analysis with ggfunction
text: survival analysis, time event interest (failure, death, relapse, etc.) described four interrelated functions: Density f(x)f(x): instantaneous rate events time xx. Survival function S(x)=1−F(x)=P(T>x)S(x) = 1 - F(x) = P(T > x): probability surviving past time xx. Hazard function h(x)=f(x)/S(x)h(x) = f(x) / S(x): instantaneous failure rate time xx, conditional survival point. Cumulative hazard function H(x)=∫0xh(t)dt=−log⁡S(x)H(x) = \int_0^x h(t)\,dt = -\log S(x): accumulated hazard time xx. four functions equivalent representations distribution: knowing one determines three. ggfunction provides geoms four, theoretical distributions empirical data.

title: Survival Analysis with ggfunction
text: geom_survival() draws S(x)=1−F(x)S(x) = 1 - F(x) CDF survival function. Different distributions encode different assumptions failure mechanism.  exponential survival curve (blue) decays constant rate—memoryless property. Weibull (red) decays faster time (increasing hazard), normal (green) concentrates failures around mean.
code: ggplot() +   geom_survival(cdf_fun = pexp,   xlim = c(0, 6), args = list(rate = 1),                 colour = "steelblue") +   geom_survival(cdf_fun = pweibull, xlim = c(0, 6),                 args = list(shape = 2, scale = 2), colour = "firebrick") +   geom_survival(cdf_fun = pnorm, xlim = c(-3, 6),                 args = list(mean = 3, sd = 1), colour = "forestgreen") +   labs(x = "Time", y = "S(x)",        title = "Survival functions for three distributions") +   theme_minimal() #> Warning: Duplicated aesthetics after name standardisation: colour #> Duplicated aesthetics after name standardisation: colour #> Duplicated aesthetics after name standardisation: colour

title: Survival Analysis with ggfunction
text: hazard function h(x)=f(x)/S(x)h(x) = f(x) / S(x) reveals instantaneous risk failure time point. shape signature underlying failure mechanism.  three canonical shapes : constant (exponential, blue—aging), increasing (Weibull shape > 1, red—wear-failures), decreasing (Weibull shape < 1, green—infant mortality). Real-world systems often exhibit “bathtub curve” combines three phases.
code: ggplot() +   geom_hf(pdf_fun = dexp, cdf_fun = pexp, xlim = c(0.01, 6),           args = list(rate = 1), colour = "steelblue") +   geom_hf(pdf_fun = dweibull, cdf_fun = pweibull, xlim = c(0.01, 6),           args = list(shape = 2, scale = 2), colour = "firebrick") +   geom_hf(pdf_fun = dweibull, cdf_fun = pweibull, xlim = c(0.01, 6),           args = list(shape = 0.5, scale = 2), colour = "forestgreen") +   labs(x = "Time", y = "h(x)",        title = "Three canonical hazard shapes") +   theme_minimal() #> Warning: Duplicated aesthetics after name standardisation: colour #> Duplicated aesthetics after name standardisation: colour #> Duplicated aesthetics after name standardisation: colour

title: Survival Analysis with ggfunction
text: cumulative hazard H(x)=−log⁡S(x)H(x) = -\log S(x) accumulates instantaneous hazard time. linear cumulative hazard corresponds constant hazard rate (exponential distribution).  exponential cumulative hazard (blue) straight line slope equal rate parameter. Weibull increasing hazard (red) curves upward, decreasing-hazard Weibull (green) curves downward.
code: ggplot() +   geom_chf(cdf_fun = pexp, xlim = c(0, 6), args = list(rate = 1),            colour = "steelblue") +   geom_chf(cdf_fun = pweibull, xlim = c(0, 6),            args = list(shape = 2, scale = 2), colour = "firebrick") +   geom_chf(cdf_fun = pweibull, xlim = c(0, 6),            args = list(shape = 0.5, scale = 2), colour = "forestgreen") +   labs(x = "Time", y = "H(x)",        title = "Cumulative hazard functions") +   theme_minimal()

title: Survival Analysis with ggfunction
text: four functions together provide complete characterization distribution. display four Weibull(2, 2) distribution.
code: args_w <- list(shape = 2, scale = 2) xl <- c(0.01, 6)  p_pdf <- ggplot() +   geom_pdf(fun = dweibull, xlim = xl, args = args_w) +   labs(x = "x", y = "f(x)", title = "Density") +   theme_minimal()  p_surv <- ggplot() +   geom_survival(cdf_fun = pweibull, xlim = xl, args = args_w) +   labs(x = "x", y = "S(x)", title = "Survival") +   theme_minimal()  p_haz <- ggplot() +   geom_hf(pdf_fun = dweibull, cdf_fun = pweibull, xlim = xl, args = args_w) +   labs(x = "x", y = "h(x)", title = "Hazard") +   theme_minimal()  p_chf <- ggplot() +   geom_chf(cdf_fun = pweibull, xlim = xl, args = args_w) +   labs(x = "x", y = "H(x)", title = "Cumulative hazard") +   theme_minimal()  (p_pdf | p_surv) / (p_haz | p_chf)

title: Survival Analysis with ggfunction
text: working observed data, geom_echf() computes empirical cumulative hazard function transforming empirical CDF: Ĥn(x)=−log⁡(1−F̂n(x)).\hat{H}_n(x) = -\log(1 - \hat{F}_n(x)). final observation, F̂n(x)=1\hat{F}_n(x) = 1 Ĥn=∞\hat{H}_n = \infty, automatically dropped, step function extends right panel edge last finite value. simultaneous confidence band derived applying monotone transformation H=−log⁡(1−F)H = -\log(1 - F) DKW bounds CDF. Since DKW inequality guarantees P(supx|F̂n(x)−F(x)|≤ε)≥1−α,P\!\left(\sup_x |\hat{F}_n(x) - F(x)| \leq \varepsilon\right) \geq 1 - \alpha, transformed band [−log⁡(1−(F̂n(x)−ε)),−log⁡(1−(F̂n(x)+ε))][-\log(1 - (\hat{F}_n(x) - \varepsilon)),\; -\log(1 - (\hat{F}_n(x) + \varepsilon))] covers true H(x)H(x) simultaneously xx probability.
code: set.seed(42) df <- data.frame(x = rexp(50, rate = 0.5))  ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +   geom_echf() +   labs(x = "Time", y = expression(hat(H)[n](x)),        title = "Empirical cumulative hazard (Exp(0.5) data)") +   theme_minimal() #> Upper confidence band clipped at "H" = 4.61 (`log(2n)`); true upper bound is #> infinite where `F_n(x) + eps >= 1`. Set `band_max = Inf` to disable.

title: Survival Analysis with ggfunction
text: Overlaying geom_chf() geom_echf() provides visual goodness--fit check: theoretical cumulative hazard lies within confidence band, data consistent model.  theoretical line (red) threads confidence band, confirming data consistent Exp(0.5)\text{Exp}(0.5) model. exponential distribution, cumulative hazard linear: H(x)=λxH(x) = \lambda x, plot analogous log-survival plot straight line confirms exponentiality.
code: set.seed(42) df <- data.frame(x = rexp(80, rate = 0.5))  ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +   geom_echf(show_points = FALSE, show_vert = FALSE) +   geom_chf(cdf_fun = pexp, xlim = c(0, max(df$x)),            args = list(rate = 0.5), colour = "red") +   labs(x = "Time", y = "H(x)",        title = "Empirical CHF vs. Exp(0.5) theory") +   theme_minimal() #> Upper confidence band clipped at "H" = 5.08 (`log(2n)`); true upper bound is #> infinite where `F_n(x) + eps >= 1`. Set `band_max = Inf` to disable.

title: Survival Analysis with ggfunction
text: geom_echf() supports grouped data via colour aesthetic, enabling side--side comparison cumulative hazard patterns populations.  high-risk group’s cumulative hazard rises steeply, low-risk group’s rises gradually. non-overlapping confidence bands provide visual evidence two groups different hazard profiles.
code: set.seed(7) df_groups <- data.frame(   x     = c(rexp(60, rate = 1), rexp(60, rate = 0.3)),   group = rep(c("High risk (rate=1)", "Low risk (rate=0.3)"), each = 60) )  ggplot(df_groups, aes(x = x, colour = group)) +   geom_echf(show_points = FALSE, show_vert = FALSE) +   labs(x = "Time", y = expression(hat(H)[n](x)),        colour = NULL,        title = "Comparing cumulative hazard across groups") +   theme_minimal() #> Upper confidence band clipped at "H" = 4.79 (`log(2n)`); true upper bound is #> infinite where `F_n(x) + eps >= 1`. Set `band_max = Inf` to disable. #> Upper confidence band clipped at "H" = 4.79 (`log(2n)`); true upper bound is #> infinite where `F_n(x) + eps >= 1`. Set `band_max = Inf` to disable.

title: Survival Analysis with ggfunction
text: practice, survival data often right-censored: subjects leave study event observed, know event time exceeds censoring time. Kaplan-Meier estimator handles adjusting risk set event time: Ŝ(t)=∏tj≤t(1−djnj),\hat{S}(t) = \prod_{t_j \le t} \left(1 - \frac{d_j}{n_j}\right), djd_j number events time tjt_j njn_j number risk just tjt_j. geom_ecdf_km() requires two aesthetics: x (observed time) status (1 = event, 0 = censored). draws KM curve decreasing step function, simultaneous equal-precision Greenwood confidence band, “+” marks censoring times.  Overlaying theoretical survival curve. empirical CHF, can overlay geom_survival() assess goodness fit.  Grouped data. Map colour grouping variable compare survival across populations.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 60 true_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.5) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.2) df_cens <- data.frame(   time   = pmin(true_time, cens_time),   status = as.integer(true_time <= cens_time) )  ggplot(df_cens, aes(x = time, status = status)) +   geom_ecdf_km() +   labs(x = "Time", y = expression(hat(S)(t)),        title = "Kaplan-Meier survival curve") +   theme_minimal() ggplot(df_cens, aes(x = time, status = status)) +   geom_ecdf_km(show_points = FALSE, show_vert = FALSE) +   geom_survival(cdf_fun = pexp, xlim = c(0, max(df_cens$time)),                 args = list(rate = 0.5), colour = "red") +   labs(x = "Time", y = expression(hat(S)(t)),        title = "KM curve vs. Exp(0.5) theory") +   theme_minimal() #> Warning: Duplicated aesthetics after name standardisation: #> colour set.seed(7) df_km_groups <- data.frame(   time   = c(rexp(50, rate = 0.5), rexp(50, rate = 1.5)),   status = sample(0:1, 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.2, 0.8)),   group  = rep(c("Low risk", "High risk"), each = 50) )  ggplot(df_km_groups, aes(x = time, status = status, colour = group)) +   geom_ecdf_km(show_points = FALSE, show_vert = FALSE) +   labs(x = "Time", y = expression(hat(S)(t)),        colour = NULL,        title = "Kaplan-Meier curves by group") +   theme_minimal()

title: Survival Analysis with ggfunction
text: Nelson-Aalen estimator censored-data analogue empirical cumulative hazard: Ĥ(t)=∑tj≤tdjnj.\hat{H}(t) = \sum_{t_j \le t} \frac{d_j}{n_j}. geom_echf_na() renders increasing step function pointwise normal confidence band based variance estimator Var̂[Ĥ(t)]=∑tj≤tdj/nj2\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}[\hat{H}(t)] = \sum_{t_j \le t} d_j / n_j^2.  Overlaying theoretical cumulative hazard.  Grouped data.
code: ggplot(df_cens, aes(x = time, status = status)) +   geom_echf_na() +   labs(x = "Time", y = expression(hat(H)(t)),        title = "Nelson-Aalen cumulative hazard") +   theme_minimal() ggplot(df_cens, aes(x = time, status = status)) +   geom_echf_na(show_points = FALSE, show_vert = FALSE) +   geom_chf(cdf_fun = pexp, xlim = c(0, max(df_cens$time)),            args = list(rate = 0.5), colour = "red") +   labs(x = "Time", y = expression(hat(H)(t)),        title = "Nelson-Aalen vs. Exp(0.5) theory") +   theme_minimal() ggplot(df_km_groups, aes(x = time, status = status, colour = group)) +   geom_echf_na(show_points = FALSE, show_vert = FALSE) +   labs(x = "Time", y = expression(hat(H)(t)),        colour = NULL,        title = "Nelson-Aalen cumulative hazard by group") +   theme_minimal()

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: ggfunction extends ggplot2 geoms stats plotting mathematical functions probability distributions directly function objects. Supply function domain; ggfunction handles evaluation, rendering, shading.

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: package organized around four families geoms:

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: geom_function_1d_1d() evaluates function f:ℝ→ℝf\colon \mathbb{R} \\mathbb{R} n equally-spaced points xlim draws curve, extending ggplot2::stat_function() built-region shading. following example plots sine function one full period.  Shading intervals. shade_from shade_to parameters fill region curve xx-axis specified interval. shade sine function −1-1 11.
code: library("ggfunction")  ggplot() +   geom_function_1d_1d(fun = sin, xlim = c(0, 2*pi)) ggplot() +   geom_function_1d_1d(fun = sin, xlim = c(-3, 3), shade_from = -1, shade_to = 1)

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: geom_function_1d_2d() evaluates parametric curve 𝜸(t)=(x(t),y(t))\boldsymbol{\gamma}(t) = (x(t),\, y(t)) range tlim step size dt, coloring path time parameter default. following example traces lemniscate Bernoulli – figure-eight curve along product distances two foci constant – 𝜸(t)=(cos⁡t/(1+sin⁡2t),sin⁡tcos⁡t/(1+sin⁡2t))\boldsymbol{\gamma}(t) = \bigl(\cos t\,/\,(1 + \sin^2 t),\; \sin t\cos t\,/\,(1 + \sin^2 t)\bigr).  Arrowheads tail points. Setting tail_point = TRUE passing grid::arrow() arrow marks starting position curve point tail arrowhead, respectively, useful showing initial condition direction curve.  Parameterized families. Extra parameters fun passed via args. following example traces Lissajous figure, closed curve results two sinusoids rational frequency ratio combined.
code: lemniscate <- function(t) c(cos(t) / (1 + sin(t)^2), sin(t) * cos(t) / (1 + sin(t)^2))  ggplot() +   geom_function_1d_2d(fun = lemniscate, tlim = c(0, 1.9 * pi)) ggplot() +   geom_function_1d_2d(     fun = lemniscate, tlim = c(0, 1.9 * pi), tail_point = TRUE,     arrow = grid::arrow(angle = 30, length = grid::unit(0.02, "npc"), type = "closed")   ) lissajous <- function(t, a = 3, b = 2, delta = pi/2) {   c(sin(a * t + delta), sin(b * t)) }  ggplot() +   geom_function_1d_2d(     fun = lissajous, tlim = c(0, 1.9 * pi),     args = list(a = 3, b = 2, delta = pi/2)   )

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: geom_function_2d_1d() evaluates scalar field f:ℝ2→ℝf\colon \mathbb{R}^2 \\mathbb{R} n×nn \times n grid xlim ×\timesylim. function must accept numeric vector length 2 return scalar; extracting components x <- v[1]; y <- v[2] recommended pattern. example renders f(x,y)=sin⁡(2πx)+sin⁡(2πy)f(x,y) = \sin(2\pi x) + \sin(2\pi y) raster heatmap (default type).  Alpha rasters. default raster maps function values fill, yielding usual continuous fill scale legend. opacity-based displays, set raster_aes = "alpha" keep fixed dark-gray fill scale finite values literal 0–1 alpha values.  Contour modes. type argument switches among three visual encodings field. "contour" draws iso-level curves colored level value; "contour_filled" draws filled regions levels; respond bins, binwidth, breaks.   Parameterized fields. function geoms, extra parameters fun passed via args.
code: f <- function(v) {   x <- v[1]; y <- v[2]   sin(2*pi*x) + sin(2*pi*y) }  ggplot() +   geom_function_2d_1d(fun = f, xlim = c(-1, 1), ylim = c(-1, 1)) h <- function(v) exp(-(v[1]^2 + v[2]^2) / 2)  ggplot() +   geom_function_2d_1d(     fun = h,     xlim = c(-4, 4),     ylim = c(-4, 4),     raster_aes = "alpha"   ) +   coord_equal() ggplot() +   geom_function_2d_1d(fun = f, xlim = c(-1, 1), ylim = c(-1, 1), type = "contour") ggplot() +   geom_function_2d_1d(fun = f, xlim = c(-1, 1), ylim = c(-1, 1), type = "contour_filled") g <- function(v, a = 1, b = 1) a * sin(v[1]) + b * cos(v[2])  ggplot() +   geom_function_2d_1d(fun = g, xlim = c(-5, 5), ylim = c(-5, 5), args = list(a = 2, b = 0.5))

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: geom_function_2d_2d() visualizes vector field 𝑭:ℝ2→ℝ2\mathbf{F}\colon \mathbb{R}^2 \\mathbb{R}^2 via ggvfields. function accept length-2 vector return length-2 vector. default (type = "vector") draws short arrows grid point colored field magnitude; type = "stream" switches integral-curve streamlines. following example shows rotation field 𝑭(x,y)=(−y,x)\mathbf{F}(x,y) = (-y,\, x).  Stream field. Setting type = "stream" renders field integral curves computed numerical integration, colored average speed along curve.
code: f <- function(u) {   x <- u[1]; y <- u[2]   c(-y, x) }  ggplot() +   geom_function_2d_2d(fun = f, xlim = c(-1, 1), ylim = c(-1, 1)) ggplot() +   geom_function_2d_2d(fun = f, xlim = c(-1, 1), ylim = c(-1, 1), type = "stream")

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: probability family provides geom standard functions associated distribution. accepts native function type via fun (e.g. dnorm geom_pdf(), pnorm geom_cdf()), also accept alternate function types converted internally—example, geom_cdf(pdf_fun = dnorm) derives CDF numerical integration, geom_pdf(hf_fun = h) derives density hazard function. Distribution parameters passed via args; finite-support hazards pass support origin hf_lower.

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: following examples demonstrate cross-conversion feature. CDF PDF. obtain standard normal CDF dnorm alone—call pnorm required. CDF derived numerical integration.  PDF hazard function. Given Weibull hazard function h(x)h(x), geom_pdf() recovers density via identity f(x)=h(x)exp⁡(−H(x))f(x) = h(x)\exp(-H(x)). Since Weibull support begins 0, pass hf_lower = 0 cumulative hazard integrated support origin.
code: ggplot() +   geom_cdf(pdf_fun = dnorm, xlim = c(-3, 3)) h_weibull <- function(x, shape, scale) {   (shape / scale) * (x / scale)^(shape - 1) }  ggplot() +   geom_pdf(hf_fun = h_weibull, hf_lower = 0, xlim = c(0.01, 5), args = list(shape = 2, scale = 1))

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: geom_pdf() draws probability density function filled area. validates supplied function integrates 1 xlim, warning via cli . basic call draws full density shading.  Single threshold. p parameter shades left boundary pp-quantile (lower.tail = TRUE, default). Setting lower.tail = FALSE shades upper tail instead. shade lower 97.5%.  Two-sided interval. p_lower p_upper together shade central region two quantiles—natural picture confidence interval. following example shades central 95%.  Tail shading. Setting shade_outside = TRUE inverts two-sided region, shading tails. rejection region two-sided hypothesis test level α=0.05\alpha = 0.05.  Highest density region. shade_hdr shades smallest region domain containing specified probability mass—highest density region (HDR). multimodal densities region can disconnected. following example uses asymmetric mixture two normals; 80% HDR captures modes two disjoint intervals, area allocated taller, narrower component.  Composing distributions. Every ggfunction layer standard ggplot2 layer, multiple distributions compose freely. overlay two normal densities different means spreads.
code: ggplot() +   geom_pdf(fun = dnorm, xlim = c(-3, 3)) ggplot() +   geom_pdf(fun = dnorm, xlim = c(-3, 3), p = 0.975) ggplot() +   geom_pdf(fun = dnorm, xlim = c(-3, 3), p_lower = 0.025, p_upper = 0.975) ggplot() +   geom_pdf(     fun = dnorm, xlim = c(-3, 3),     p_lower = 0.025, p_upper = 0.975, shade_outside = TRUE   ) f_mix <- function(x) 0.6 * dnorm(x, mean = -2, sd = 0.6) + 0.4 * dnorm(x, mean = 2, sd = 1.2) ggplot() +   geom_pdf(fun = f_mix, xlim = c(-5, 6), shade_hdr = 0.8) ggplot() +   geom_pdf(fun = dnorm, xlim = c(-5, 8), alpha = 0.4) +   geom_pdf(fun = dnorm, xlim = c(-5, 8),     args = list(mean = 3, sd = 1.5), alpha = 0.4)

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: geom_pdf_2d() visualizes theoretical bivariate density highest density regions raw density raster. thin wrapper around ggdensity HDRs: function supplied ggfunction’s fun(c(x, y)) convention adapted internally, HDR computation, probability labels, default aesthetics delegated ggdensity::geom_hdr_fun() (filled regions, default type = "hdr") ggdensity::geom_hdr_lines_fun() (type = "hdr_lines"). type = "raster", density evaluated requested grid drawn alpha-raster mode geom_function_2d_1d(): dark-gray fill alpha scaled literal 0–1 transparency. Parameters pass via args usual.  HDR contour lines. type = "hdr_lines" draws region boundaries. correlated covariance matrix passed via args.  Raw density raster. type = "raster" plots evaluated density grid directly. default fill fixed dark gray, lowest finite density transparent, highest finite density opaque.  arbitrary iso-density contours calibrated probability content, use geom_function_2d_1d() type = "contour" "contour_filled".
code: dbvn <- function(v, mu = c(0, 0), Sigma = diag(2)) {   x <- matrix(v - mu, ncol = 1)   Sinv <- solve(Sigma)   1 / (2 * pi * sqrt(det(Sigma))) * exp(-0.5 * as.numeric(t(x) %*% Sinv %*% x)) }  ggplot() +   geom_pdf_2d(fun = dbvn, xlim = c(-3, 3), ylim = c(-3, 3), probs = c(0.5, 0.8, 0.95)) +   coord_equal() Sigma <- matrix(c(1, 0.6, 0.6, 1), 2, 2)  ggplot() +   geom_pdf_2d(     fun = dbvn, args = list(Sigma = Sigma),     xlim = c(-3, 3), ylim = c(-3, 3),     probs = c(0.5, 0.8, 0.95), type = "hdr_lines"   ) +   coord_equal() ggplot() +   geom_pdf_2d(fun = dbvn, xlim = c(-3, 3), ylim = c(-3, 3), type = "raster") +   coord_equal()

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: geom_pmf() evaluates probability mass function integer xlim renders result lollipop chart—vertical segments capped points. Distribution parameters supplied via args. following example plots Binomial(10,0.3)\text{Binomial}(10, 0.3) distribution.  Single threshold. p parameter shades lollipops pp-quantile (unshaded region rendered reduced opacity). shade lower 80% Binomial(10,0.5)\text{Binomial}(10, 0.5) distribution.  Highest density regions. shade_hdr accepts one coverage levels shades smallest sets support points whose total probability mass meets exceeds target. support point’s smallest containing HDR mapped alpha ordered factor legend—convention geom_pmf_2d(). symmetric unimodal PMF central intervals; skewed distribution asymmetric. 50/80/95% HDRs Binomial(10,0.3)\text{Binomial}(10, 0.3) distribution highlighted. Two properties discrete shading worth keeping mind. First, almost always overshoots: discrete distribution can rarely hit target coverage exactly, smallest region coverage least target used message reports achieved coverages (cumulative p/p_lower/p_upper intervals likewise include support point crosses threshold). Second, HDRs never split ties: HDR probability-threshold set {x:p(x)≥c}\{x : p(x) \ge c\}, support points exactly equal mass always included excluded together—another source overshoot.  Viridis HDR fill. shade_hdr maps HDR membership alpha default. alpha encoding subtle, map after_stat(probs) discrete fill scale instead fix alpha = 1: lollipop points use fillable shape, HDR colors fill points beneath black outlines. (Mapping colour instead carries HDR colors sticks outlines .)  Explicit support. support sequence consecutive integers, pass exact support points via support. plot distribution sample mean X‾\bar X 10 iid Bernoulli(0.3)\text{Bernoulli}(0.3) draws. support {0,0.1,0.2,…,1}\{0, 0.1, 0.2, \ldots, 1\} PMF binomial: P(X‾=k/10)=(10k)0.3k0.710−kP(\bar X = k/10) = \binom{10}{k}0.3^k 0.7^{10-k}.
code: ggplot() +   geom_pmf(fun = dbinom, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(size = 10, prob = 0.3)) ggplot() +   geom_pmf(fun = dbinom, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(size = 10, prob = 0.5), p = 0.8) ggplot() +   geom_pmf(fun = dbinom, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(size = 10, prob = 0.3),     shade_hdr = c(0.5, 0.8, 0.95)) ggplot() +   geom_pmf(     fun = dbinom, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(size = 10, prob = 0.3),     shade_hdr = c(0.5, 0.8, 0.95),     mapping = aes(fill = after_stat(probs)), alpha = 1   ) +   scale_fill_viridis_d() f_mean <- function(x, prob) dbinom(round(x * 10), size = 10, prob = prob) ggplot() +   geom_pmf(fun = f_mean, support = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1), args = list(prob = 0.3))

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: geom_pmf_2d() evaluates bivariate mass function discrete lattice—integers spanning xlim ×\timesylim default, exact values via support_x/support_y. function uses fun(c(x, y)) convention bivariate layers, parameters passed via args. default type = "point" renders balloon plot size encoding probability mass; scale_size_area() makes area proportional mass zero mass vanish.  Tile heatmap. type = "tile" encodes mass fill instead.  Highest density regions. shade_hdr accepts one coverage levels. lattice point assigned smallest HDR contains , assignment mapped alpha ordered factor—legend convention geom_pdf_2d()—points outside requested regions nearly transparent. Exact coverages may achievable discrete distribution, case smallest HDRs least target coverages used message reports actual coverages; univariate case, lattice points exactly equal mass always assigned region, achieved coverage almost always overshoots target.  Viridis HDR fill. alpha encoding subtle, map HDR levels fill discrete viridis palette instead, fixing alpha = 1 turn default encoding; points use fillable shape default. Probability mass stays encoded point size. fill also makes discrete-HDR properties easy see: regions overshoot targets, points nearly identical size can carry different HDR colors—masses differ slightly, exactly tied masses always share region.  Non-product support. Distributions like trinomial live simplex rather full product lattice. Evaluate bounding lattice, returning 0 support; zero-mass cells dropped default (drop_zeros = TRUE).
code: dbinom2 <- function(v, sizes = c(10, 10), probs = c(0.5, 0.5)) {   dbinom(v[1], sizes[1], probs[1]) * dbinom(v[2], sizes[2], probs[2]) }  ggplot() +   geom_pmf_2d(fun = dbinom2, xlim = c(0, 10), ylim = c(0, 10), args = list(probs = c(0.3, 0.7))) +   scale_size_area() ggplot() +   geom_pmf_2d(fun = dbinom2, xlim = c(0, 10), ylim = c(0, 10), args = list(probs = c(0.3, 0.7)),     type = "tile") ggplot() +   geom_pmf_2d(fun = dbinom2, xlim = c(0, 10), ylim = c(0, 10), shade_hdr = c(0.5, 0.8, 0.95)) +   scale_size_area() ggplot() +   geom_pmf_2d(     fun = dbinom2, xlim = c(0, 10), ylim = c(0, 10), args = list(probs = c(0.3, 0.7)),     shade_hdr = c(0.5, 0.8, 0.95),     mapping = aes(fill = after_stat(probs)), alpha = 1   ) +   scale_size_area() +   scale_fill_viridis_d() dtrinom <- function(v, size = 8, prob = c(0.3, 0.3, 0.4)) {   if (sum(v) > size) return(0)   dmultinom(c(v, size - sum(v)), prob = prob) }  ggplot() +   geom_pmf_2d(fun = dtrinom, xlim = c(0, 8), ylim = c(0, 8)) +   scale_size_area() +   coord_equal()

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: cumulative distribution function F(x)=P(X≤x)F(x) = P(X \le x) available continuous discrete distributions. Continuous (geom_cdf()). Draws CDF line. plot standard normal CDF.  Shading. Like geom_pdf(), geom_cdf() accepts p single threshold, p_lower/p_upper two-sided interval, lower.tail tail direction. shade 90th percentile.  Discrete (geom_cdf_discrete()). Takes PMF, accumulates right-continuous step-function CDF, renders horizontal segments, dashed vertical jumps, open circles lower limit jump, closed circles achieved value. PMF-derived CDFs, support full computational support xlim filters displayed range. following example plots Poisson(5)\text{Poisson}(5) CDF.  Hiding points lines. show_points = FALSE show_vert = FALSE remove endpoint circles vertical jump segments, leaving horizontal staircase. default, points vertical jump segments shown 50 fewer support points; larger supports hidden automatically.  Explicit support. Pass full set non-integer support points directly via support. plot CDF sample mean 10 iid Bernoulli(0.3)\text{Bernoulli}(0.3) draws.
code: ggplot() +   geom_cdf(fun = pnorm, xlim = c(-3, 3)) ggplot() +   geom_cdf(fun = pnorm, xlim = c(-3, 3), p = 0.9) ggplot() +   geom_cdf_discrete(pmf_fun = dpois, xlim = c(0, 15), args = list(lambda = 5)) ggplot() +   geom_cdf_discrete(pmf_fun = dbinom, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(size = 10, prob = 0.5),     show_points = FALSE, show_vert = FALSE) f_mean <- function(x, prob) dbinom(round(x * 10), size = 10, prob = prob) ggplot() +   geom_cdf_discrete(pmf_fun = f_mean, support = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1), args = list(prob = 0.3))

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: survival function S(x)=1−F(x)=P(X>x)S(x) = 1 - F(x) = P(X > x) gives probability surviving past xx. Continuous (geom_survival()). Accepts survival function directly via fun, derives SS CDF via cdf_fun, PDF via pdf_fun, quantile function via qf_fun. following example shows survival function Exponential(0.5)\text{Exponential}(0.5) distribution, decays S(x)=e−0.5xS(x) = e^{-0.5x}.  Discrete (geom_survival_discrete()). Takes PMF renders S(x)=1−F(x)S(x) = 1 - F(x) right-continuous step function using visual conventions geom_cdf_discrete(). discrete CDF, PMF-derived survival curves require full computational support. following example plots Binomial(10,0.5)\text{Binomial}(10, 0.5) survival function.  Hiding points. Setting show_points = FALSE removes endpoint circles.  Explicit support. survival function sample mean 10 iid Bernoulli(0.3)\text{Bernoulli}(0.3) draws.
code: ggplot() +   geom_survival(cdf_fun = pexp, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(rate = 0.5)) ggplot() +   geom_survival_discrete(pmf_fun = dbinom, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(size = 10, prob = 0.5)) ggplot() +   geom_survival_discrete(pmf_fun = dbinom, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(size = 10, prob = 0.5),     show_points = FALSE) f_mean <- function(x, prob) dbinom(round(x * 10), size = 10, prob = prob) ggplot() +   geom_survival_discrete(pmf_fun = f_mean, support = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1), args = list(prob = 0.3))

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: quantile function Q(p)=inf⁡{x:F(x)≥p}Q(p) = \inf\{x : F(x) \ge p\} inverts CDF. Continuous (geom_qf()). Evaluates quantile function unit interval (0,1)(0, 1) draws curve. following example plots standard normal quantile function.  Discrete (geom_qf_discrete()). Takes PMF renders quantile function left-continuous step function unit interval, closed circles bottom jump (value achieved) open circles top (next value yet reached). PMF-derived quantile functions inverted CDF computed full support. following example plots Binomial(10,0.5)\text{Binomial}(10, 0.5) quantile function.  Parameterized families. plot Poisson(5)\text{Poisson}(5) discrete quantile function.  Hiding points. Setting show_points = FALSE removes endpoint circles, leaving horizontal vertical lines.  Hiding vertical lines. Setting show_vert = FALSE removes dashed vertical jump segments, leaving horizontal steps endpoint circles.  Explicit support. quantile function sample mean 10 iid Bernoulli(0.3)\text{Bernoulli}(0.3) draws.
code: ggplot() +   geom_qf(fun = qnorm) ggplot() +   geom_qf_discrete(pmf_fun = dbinom, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(size = 10, prob = 0.5)) ggplot() +   geom_qf_discrete(pmf_fun = dpois, xlim = c(0, 15), args = list(lambda = 5)) ggplot() +   geom_qf_discrete(pmf_fun = dbinom, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(size = 10, prob = 0.5),     show_points = FALSE) ggplot() +   geom_qf_discrete(pmf_fun = dbinom, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(size = 10, prob = 0.5),     show_vert = FALSE) f_mean <- function(x, prob) dbinom(round(x * 10), size = 10, prob = prob) ggplot() +   geom_qf_discrete(pmf_fun = f_mean, support = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1), args = list(prob = 0.3))

title: Extending ggplot2 with functional capabilities
text: geom_hf() plots hazard function h(x)=f(x)/S(x)h(x) = f(x) / S(x), instantaneous rate failure time xx conditional survival point. accepts hazard function directly via fun, derives hh pdf_fun /cdf_fun (one provided, derived numerically). Shared parameters go args; use pdf_args cdf_args overrides two functions different parameterizations. exponential distribution’s constant hazard confirms memoryless property.  Canonical hazard shapes. Three canonical shapes arise reliability modeling: decreasing (infant mortality), constant (memoryless), increasing (wear-). Weibull distribution shape < 1 gives decreasing hazard; normal distribution’s hazard eventually increasing.
code: ggplot() +   geom_hf(pdf_fun = dexp, cdf_fun = pexp, xlim = c(0.01, 10), args = list(rate = 0.5)) library(patchwork)  p_decr <- ggplot() +   geom_hf(pdf_fun = dweibull, cdf_fun = pweibull,     xlim = c(0.01, 5), args = list(shape = 0.5, scale = 2)) +   ggtitle("Decreasing (Weibull)")  p_incr <- ggplot() +   geom_hf(pdf_fun = dnorm, cdf_fun = pnorm,     xlim = c(-3, 3)) +   ggtitle("Increasing (Normal)")  p_decr | p_incr

Overlaying theoretical survival curve Kaplan-Meier estimate provides visual check: curve lies within Greenwood EP confidence band, data consistent model. overlay true Exp(0.5)\text{Exp}(0.5) survival function.

title: Plot a Kaplan-Meier Survival Curve for Censored Data — geom_ecdf_km
text: Kaplan-Meier estimator event time \(t_j\) $$\hat{S}(t) = \prod_{t_j \le t} \left(1 - \frac{d_j}{n_j}\right),$$ \(d_j\) number events \(n_j\) number risk just \(t_j\). simultaneous confidence band uses Greenwood variance estimator $$\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}[\hat{S}(t)] = \hat{S}(t)^2 \sum_{t_j \le t} \frac{d_j}{n_j(n_j - d_j)}$$ equal-precision (EP) critical value Nair (1984), giving bounds \(\hat{S}(t) \pm c_{\mathrm{EP}}\,\mathrm{se}(t)\) clipped \([0, 1]\). EP critical value \(c_{\mathrm{EP}}\) derived asymptotic distribution standardized KM process. depends endpoints \((t) = nG(t)/(1 + nG(t))\), \(G(t)\) Greenwood's cumulative variance term, evaluated first last valid event-time values. resulting band simultaneous (valid \(t\) jointly), merely pointwise, asymptotically correct.

text: empirical quantile function left-continuous inverse empirical CDF: \(Q(p) = \inf\{x : F_n(x) \geq p\}\). two-sided confidence band probability level \(p\) \([Q_n(p - \varepsilon),\, Q_n(p + \varepsilon)]\), \(\varepsilon = \sqrt{\log(2/\alpha) / (2n)}\) DKW/Massart half-width (\(\alpha = 1 - \texttt{level}\)). extreme tails, DKW gives one-sided bounds unless known support bounds supplied; ribbon displays open-ended, panel-clipped tails. follows directly inverting simultaneous ECDF confidence band.

Sinusoidal combination of sine and cosine f_sin_cos <- function(v) {   x <- v[1]   y <- v[2]   sin(x) * cos(y) }  ggplot() +   geom_function_2d_1d(fun = f_sin_cos, xlim = c(-5, 5), ylim = c(-5, 5))   # Spiral pattern function f_spiral <- function(v) {   x <- v[1]   y <- v[2]   r <- sqrt(x^2 + y^2)   theta <- atan2(y, x)   sin(r + theta) }  ggplot() +   geom_function_2d_1d(fun = f_spiral, xlim = c(-50, 50), ylim = c(-50, 50), n = 500)   # code: dbvn <- function(v, mu = c(0, 0), Sigma = diag(2)) {   x <- matrix(v - mu, ncol = 1)   Sinv <- solve(Sigma)   1 / (2 * pi * sqrt(det(Sigma))) *     exp(-0.5 * as.numeric(t(x) %*% Sinv %*% x)) }  ggplot() +   geom_pdf_2d(     fun = dbvn,     xlim = c(-3, 3),     ylim = c(-3, 3),     probs = c(0.5, 0.8, 0.95)   ) +   coord_equal()   ggplot() +   geom_pdf_2d(     fun = dbvn,     xlim = c(-3, 3),     ylim = c(-3, 3),     probs = c(0.5, 0.8, 0.95),     type = "hdr_lines"   ) +   coord_equal()   # Parameterized via `args` Sigma <- matrix(c(1, 0.6, 0.6, 1), 2, 2) ggplot() +   geom_pdf_2d(     fun = dbvn,     args = list(Sigma = Sigma),     xlim = c(-3, 3),     ylim = c(-3, 3),     type = "hdr_lines"   ) +   coord_equal()   ggplot() +   geom_pdf_2d(fun = dbvn, xlim = c(-3, 3), ylim = c(-3, 3), type = "raster") +   coord_equal()

code: # Independent product binomial dbinom2 <- function(v, sizes = c(10, 10), probs = c(0.5, 0.5)) {   dbinom(v[1], sizes[1], probs[1]) * dbinom(v[2], sizes[2], probs[2]) }  ggplot() +   geom_pmf_2d(fun = dbinom2, xlim = c(0, 10), ylim = c(0, 10)) +   scale_size_area()   # Parameterized via `args` ggplot() +   geom_pmf_2d(     fun = dbinom2, xlim = c(0, 10), ylim = c(0, 10),     args = list(probs = c(0.3, 0.7))   ) +   scale_size_area()   # Tile heatmap ggplot() +   geom_pmf_2d(fun = dbinom2, xlim = c(0, 10), ylim = c(0, 10),     type = "tile")   # Highlight the 50/80/95% highest density regions ggplot() +   geom_pmf_2d(fun = dbinom2, xlim = c(0, 10), ylim = c(0, 10),     shade_hdr = c(0.5, 0.8, 0.95)) +   scale_size_area() #> ! shade_hdr: exact coverage is not achievable for this discrete distribution. #> ℹ Using smallest HDRs with coverage >= each target: 50% -> 54.6%, 80% -> 83.6%, #>   95% -> 95.9%.   # Non-product support: trinomial over a bounding lattice dtrinom <- function(v, size = 8, prob = c(0.3, 0.3, 0.4)) {   if (sum(v) > size) return(0)   dmultinom(c(v, size - sum(v)), prob = prob) }  ggplot() +   geom_pmf_2d(fun = dtrinom, xlim = c(0, 8), ylim = c(0, 8)) +   scale_size_area() +   coord_equal()

title: PP, QQ, and Stabilized Probability Diagnostic Plots — geom_ppplot
text: Suppose \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) compared fully specified null distribution \(F_0\), quantile function \(Q_0 = F_0^{\leftarrow}\). Let \(x_{(1)} \le \cdots \le x_{(n)}\) denote sample order statistics let \(p_i\) plotting positions returned stats::ppoints(), default \(p_i = (- 0.5) / n\). probability-probability (PP) plot displays \(F_0(x_{()})\) \(p_i\). quantile-quantile (QQ) plot displays \(x_{()}\) \(Q_0(p_i)\). stabilized probability (SP) plot applies Michael's arcsine-square-root variance-stabilizing transform $$   g(p) = \frac{2}{\pi}\sin^{-1}\sqrt{p} $$ PP coordinates, displaying \(g\{F_0(x_{()})\}\) \(g(p_i)\). case, agreement null model represented identity line \(y = x\). Michael (1983) discusses acceptance regions PP, QQ, stabilized probability plots. confidence band based probability integral transform: \(H_0: F_X = F_0\), transformed observations \(U_i = F_0(X_i)\) iid \(\mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)\). Dvoretzky–Kiefer–Wolfowitz/Massart inequality gives, \(\alpha = 1 - \mathrm{level}\), $$   \varepsilon_{n,\alpha} =   \sqrt{\frac{\log(2/\alpha)}{2n}} . $$ Thus PP band drawn probability scale $$   \max(0, p - \varepsilon_{n,\alpha})   \le F_0(x_{()}) \le   \min(1, p + \varepsilon_{n,\alpha}), $$ SP band obtained applying \(g(\cdot)\) probability coordinates probability limits, QQ band obtained transforming probability limits back data scale: $$   Q_0\{\max(0, p - \varepsilon_{n,\alpha})\}   \le x_{()} \le   Q_0\{\min(1, p + \varepsilon_{n,\alpha})\}. $$ QQ ribbons evaluated finite probability grid reaches halfway extreme plotting positions toward 0 1, ribbon extends slightly beyond observed points theoretical-quantile axis. geom_qqplot(), ribbon coordinates drawn scale training, default x y scales determined QQ points rather ribbon tails. Points far identity line, especially points outside band, visual evidence specified null distribution. band finite-sample distribution-free fully specified \(F_0\); parameters estimated data, display best interpreted informal diagnostic. DKW bands known conservative, power can expected low.

title: PP, QQ, and Stabilized Probability Diagnostic Plots — geom_ppplot
code: set.seed(1) df <- data.frame(x = rnorm(50))  ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +   geom_ppplot(fun = pnorm) +   coord_equal()   ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +   geom_spplot(fun = pnorm) +   coord_equal()   ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +   geom_qqplot(fun = qnorm) +   coord_equal()   # Parameterized null distribution via `args` df2 <- data.frame(x = rnorm(50, mean = 2, sd = 2)) ggplot(df2, aes(x = x)) +   geom_ppplot(fun = pnorm, args = list(mean = 2, sd = 2)) +   coord_equal()   # Use fixed black points by setting a fixed colour. ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +   geom_qqplot(fun = qnorm, colour = "black") +   coord_equal()   # Or add a spectral/rainbow colour scale explicitly. ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +   geom_qqplot(fun = qnorm) +   scale_colour_gradientn(colors = grDevices::rainbow(10)) +   coord_equal()

Drawing aesthetics alpha, colour/color, fill, group, linetype, linewidth, shape, size, stroke steps, jump segments, endpoints.

title: Plot a Discrete Survival Function as a Step Function — geom_survival_discrete
code: # via PMF   ggplot() +     geom_survival_discrete(pmf_fun = dbinom, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(size = 10, prob = 0.5))     # via CDF   ggplot() +     geom_survival_discrete(cdf_fun = pbinom, xlim = c(0, 10), args = list(size = 10, prob = 0.5))     ggplot() +     geom_survival_discrete(pmf_fun = dpois, xlim = c(0, 15), args = list(lambda = 5))
